speaker: interviewer
text: This is just-- We're just doing bars. You can just ID it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This is July 7, 1995. We're interviewing Magda Mautner Marx. This is tape three. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut. Cut for one second, please. Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a-- the woman ran out and came back-- pale. Three Arrow Cross men are-- what should I do? I said, go and open the gate. Don't talk to them. Let them in, and don't talk. I will talk. I don't know. I was so calm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came in, three of them. And they say, we got the report that here Jews are hiding. I said, no. Nobody is here-- only we two. Nobody is hiding here. Then he said, then-- then you come with this. I said, why should I come with you? And he said, do you have some papers? So I said, yes that is on the-- I was in bed-- on the table. Give me my pocketbook-- this-- this way. And I give him the paper. And he say-- he said, then you dress, and you come with me. And I said, why? I said-- he said, because you are a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, Jew-- Jewish is your goddamn godmother-- grandmother. I just run away now from the Russians and-- and you want to-- go to-- go to hell! And they started to talk to each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know what to do, because how dare if I am Jewish. How dare I to talk to them this way. And he said, we will enter. We will go after this thing, you know? And we'll come back tomorrow. I said, go to hell, again. And they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that they left, we both-- we got such a-- we were shaking like this-- the-- the woman also. And I was surprised why she is so-- so shaken.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, OK. She didn't sleep all night. I didn't sleep all night. You can imagine. And the next morning, early morning, maybe it was 9 o'clock, again, somebody rung the bell. The woman-- I forgot her name-- goes again and then come back. Rosie is at the-- at the gate, and she is a big, big Nazi. And what should I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you cannot do anything. You let her in. And in the meantime, I hid under the bed. It was so dirty. I don't know-- [LAUGHS] maybe weeks she didn't clean under the bed. We-- she had such a big dog. And this Rosie came in. And I was under the bed. And the dog didn't know what happened-- climbed under the bed and cried. I couldn't-- I couldn't say a word or nothing. I didn't-- I couldn't move. And then the woman asked, 'Come'. I don't know what was the name of the dog. 'Come! Come here.' And the dog came out, and again, came under the bed and-- and cried there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a threat that this Rosie will look what is the dog-- [LAUGHS] dog is looking under the bed. Why-- why is he crying? But then this woman got a little safely intelligent, and said, listen, Rosie, let's go to the kitchen. I make a tea. It's so cold here in the room. So they left the-- and they-- she took the dog also. Then I was-- I stay under the bed. But-- then she left. And then I came out of the bed. And it happened a very funny thing. Suddenly, the closet door opened, and a man came out from the closet. So this was why she was so shaken and so afraid. This was the-- the boyfriend, or-- or common-law husband, or something like this. And she-- she hide-- she hid this man.

speaker: interviewee
text: He left the-- she-- he was also in a labor camp, and he escaped, and came-- came here. And this woman hid him. This is what she didn't want to-- not because she was a bad person, she didn't want to let me in, because she didn't want to injure-- endanger the man.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- after Rosie left-- so I didn't want to stay there. Because maybe these-- the Niloshes, these [NON-ENGLISH] the [NON-ENGLISH] teenagers will come back. And the-- the man went up. There was an empty apartment in the first floor. So they went up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know when he came in. Maybe I was washing up or something. In the meantime, these men came in, and he did a-- a class act I didn't [LAUGHS] know about. So-- and then I-- I left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the stair-- now where-- where should I go now? So I remembered that when I was a-- a child, we went to a small town, Budakeszi. They had-- you know what is a Schwabe? This is German-speaking-- German, but Hungarian citizen-- a whole town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I was a-- a little girl, my parents-- with the whole family, and with the maid, with everything-- we went to this town for the summer. And there, up in the mountains, it was a-- under mountains. And up in the mountains was a-- a sanatorium for tuberculotic people-- persons.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- so I go there. That is up on the mountain. Maybe there are sick people, and doctors, and nurses. So maybe they don't care for all these political things.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- with Irene, had a little booklet, you know. Because in Hungary, somebody who worked in households had to have a little booklet, and the-- the people who they were serving, they wrote in the name, and then everything, and how they were satisfied. And I took this also.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I went in. And I ask who hiring here-- personnel. So I went to this office. And I told I-- I am a refugee. I learned the-- the Russians came in to my town. And I run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- I want-- I have to work, because I have very little money, and I need work. I want to work. I need shelter. I need food. And I am willing to work. And she said-- looked at me. I said, I don't know what kind of work I can offer you, because I see that you are an intelligent person. So I thought, no more this-- this that I am household help. This goes to the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I right away, in-- in a second, I had to change my whole story. I said, yes. I am a teacher, but it doesn't meant that I take any job what you want. I-- I can sew on the sewing machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this we have it not. But I have only one-- one opening. And this is like a girl Friday, you know, the-- to substitute-- always a cleaning woman who has the day off. And it is only cleaning job. I said, OK. I-- I don't mind. And so I got this job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was-- the sanatorium was a very big building. But there was one little building standing next to it, the D Pavilion. So at first, I was in the big building, and it was very, very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- somehow, whatever I did, I didn't fit in. So I had to keep to this, that I am a-- that I am a teacher. Because still, I didn't look like a cleaning woman, whatever I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a few days there. And they-- I-- they gave me some gray clothes, like a jumper. I didn't have a-- I didn't have underwear. I didn't have anything with me. I just jump out from the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so I-- I started to work. And still, there were a few women. They had to wear the yellow star in the sanatorium. They were put out a yellow star on their door. And I had to go to clean there. And when I came in, they were talking. And when I came in, they stopped to talk. I tell you, I could cry in this minute, because they were afraid of me. And I told them. I cleaned the-- the room. And I told them, listen to me. I-- I have to go to the city. If you want to send some letters, or whatever, I gladly take it. No, no no, no no. We-- so they were afraid of me. And there were still two children, a little boy and that little girl, in a separate room, also tuberculotic. And we're forbidden to give them milk, or butter, or-- or something-- you know, nourishing food. And it was one woman from the town-- Schwabeish And I saw she had to-- to put up the shades, you know, because it was the blackout. And I saw that she put under her apron something-- food-- and sneaked into these two children, to give them something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were-- were people, you know, who-- who were human beings. Of course, I-- I couldn't say a word. I-- I was not who I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this woman, she was a very devoted Catholic and always came to me. Come to-- come to church. Come to the service. Come to church. I didn't know what-- what I have to do in the church. So always I postponed. I said, I have today this to do and that to do. And one-- one day, a Sunday, still, come, you have to come. You have, you know, you have to pray for your mother. Because I had a story that I left my mother and my family there. And it is terrible thing if somebody is not a liar to tell every word what I said, what was a lie. Then I decided that it is a role. I am an actress, and this is a role. And I will play this role as convincingly as I can. Otherwise, I am a very bad actress, and it can-- can cost my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought, OK. I cannot postpone anymore. I go to-- to church. And I will sit next to her. When she kneels, I will kneel. When she gets up, I will get up. When she cross herself, I-- so I will-- I will watch what she's doing, and I will do the same. And the-- and so this went OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end is something that you have-- the priest goes around and they give a piece of waffle or whatever you did. This is-- you have to sip a-- sip of wine. This is Jesus blood and Jesus body or what-- flesh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this, I just couldn't do that. I started to cry. And I-- I told Ava, you see this church we have now? I think my mother, my poor mother, is-- maybe she can go to church, maybe not. But she will not know what happened to me. And I am the beloved daughter, and this hurts me very much. And I cried and left the church. So one day-- the-- this was any-- a funny story also. The-- in the sanatorium, the-- the bathroom and the showers were up on the attic. And I didn't know that it is a separate room for nurses and a separate for the cleaning personnel. So I went up to take a bath, because I felt all-- all-- all the time I felt that I am covered with bacterias. You know? So I went up. And unfortunately, I went into the room that only the nurses should go. So I was sitting in the bathtub, and somebody banged at the door. It were a man. Who is that? Why-- why did you close the door? You don't know that you're not supposed to lock the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said no. I-- I don't know. Open up right away! I jump out from the bathtub and opened up. I was naked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came in a big woman and looked at me. Who are you? I said, I am Irene from the D Pavilion. She said, yeah, you are lucky that you are not a man. Now I would know who you are. So he-- I said, I don't know what you mean, but you have very good imaginations. And you don't know that you're not supposed to use this-- this bathroom? I said, no. But certainly, I will-- will left it cleaner than I found it. And she left. So in the meantime, I got another job with the-- with this little pavilion. It was a very nice nurse, but everybody hated her, because she was always looking at what the cleaning women do. But I work like from the morning to the evening, because it was the best not to talk-- so little to talk as you can.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I told her that this-- I don't know why this-- her-- she-- this-- this nurse, why they were yelling at me. Because I incidentally used that bathroom. So she didn't say a word. The next day-- I-- this was a-- a three-story building, and I was just cleaning up in the-- upper floor. Nurse calls, Irene. Here are two people from the party. They have their own Nazi party in the sanatorium. And before that, they tried to convince me to join the party. I said no. I-- I never cared for politics, and I don't do it. But here, I have to put in something else. There were three men in the-- in the big building. And they-- they worked in the garden-- were a big garden there. And when I came down from that to the-- my building, they always, they're looking at me. And I saw that they talk to each other. So I thought that I was a young, not bad-looking girl, that they talk about [LAUGHS] me something. And I heard that these three are Polish. And they-- that-- that time, a lot of Polish people escaped to Hungary from the German. And they-- the story was that they were soldiers-- Polish soldiers. And they fought against the German. And the German army-- army came in, then they escaped to Hungary. And I don't know how they got to this sanatorium. So-- and this I put in between, because later, I come back to this. So the next day, that this happened in the bathroom, came the nurse. She had me come down here. The two people from the party want to talk to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away, I know that this woman did something-- that they went-- that he-- she is suspicious that I am Jew or something. So I-- I told-- here I come right away. I have to wash my hand, because I felt that I am very pale, you know? So I went. And the-- I little massaged my face. And in the meantime, I got back my calm. And I came down. And they told me, any-- who-- who are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Irene Gyigor. And I escaped, you know, because for my-- into my town they came, the Russian. And I didn't want to stay there. I heard not-so-good things about the Russians. So-- and I found job here, and I am here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a paper-- the-- who came were the-- were-- not the-- who were running away from German, from other towns, they got-- was a office that they had to go and to get their permit. Do you have this? I said, no. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, because I don't need it. I don't want any help. I don't need. I can work. Let them give the support to others who cannot work. This was not true, but it sounded OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So do you have papers? Yes, here you have my paper. And they left. And they took my paper with them. So I thought, what now? How do I get out of this? Without, I thought, to go away without paper, I cannot go away. If they start to interrogate me and to ask all kind of questions-- where I went to-- learned to be a teacher-- because this was a little farm kind of town-- how-- way-- how many acres my father had that he could let me-- I couldn't answer. I was not prepared for that. What should I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And before that, there were little things that you could pay for with-- for your life. We did. For instance, it was one-- one day, was some noodle with cottage cheese on it. And we were sitting around on the table. And I took the sugar and put on the noodles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, everybody around the table are watching me. I say, what are you looking at me? And they say, you know, Irene, you eat these cheese noodles like the Jews with sugar. Because the other put on some bacon-- these bacon bits. I didn't know that, you know? So I put the sugar, and I put-- put on it. And then I started to laugh. How should I know how the Jews eat the-- these noodles? I like noodle only with sugar. And I sat down, and I ate. And they see that I am so calm. This-- he went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another was another girl who liked to put out tarot cards. As I say, I don't believe in that. But they-- they all, go, go. Everybody goes. So I-- I couldn't-- I couldn't do not everybody else does. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she put out the cards, and she say, Irene, do you have a child? I say, are you crazy? I am not even married. But twice I put out the card, and always comes out that you have a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I say-- I told her, listen. What a stupid thing. First of all, I am not married. Second, the card doesn't say what is now. The card has to say what will come in the future. I-- maybe I will have-- I want to have children. Then my-- I told that I have a fiance in the front somewhere in Ukraine. And when he comes back, we will get married. And I will have children. And that was it. You know, little thing, what you are not prepared. You know, that it's like-- another occasion. Comes two girl and tells, Irene, is here a man from your town and would like to talk to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I tried to postpone this also. Once, we had to go down and to bring up from the kitchen the food for people who couldn't go up to the dining room. We had to serve in the room. I was like a-- a nurse aide and a cleaning woman at the same time. So I go down. And this girl comes. Oh, now come, come. Here is this Mr. [NON-ENGLISH], I think was his name. So I thought, what now? He will see-- in a little town everybody knows each other-- he will see right away that I am not Gyigor-- Irene Gyigor. But I had no-- any other choice-- I had to go there. So he is a good looking man, and looked at me, and looked at me, and looked at me. Are you from [NON-ENGLISH]? I said, yes. He say, I cannot remember you. I say, I didn't-- I don't remember you either.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, 10 years ago, I went to Solnik. I am a pharmacist. And you must have been a little girl that time. And I said, I suppose so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And do you know my mother and my brother, how they are? I said, oh, they are OK. I saw them before I left. And I heard that the Russian very peacefully to took over. So you can be sure that they are OK. So I was lucky. And later-- never this man wanted to-- to talk to me. And I was, so-- inside, was so grateful to him. I thought, this-- this man saw on me that I am not really Gyigor, Irene.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he must be a-- a better human being. He let me go. But it was not the case. Later, I will tell about.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and that day, when they took away my papers, I was very desperate. I didn't see any-- any way how I can get out of that. So I decided, since I don't want to discuss about that, I don't want to talk to-- well, he was a very nice woman, the nurse, we got-- as a name-- teacher, you know, I was in the same level, because we got friends-- friends, as far as-- [LAUGHS] you know? So I decided that I go down to town. I go to the movie. So I had to go through-- there was a part though the forest down to the-- to Budakeszi, to the movie. I said-- I went.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- so I go in the part-- nobody goes there in the dark. And then I feel safe. So I went down. And in the movie, they were full with German soldiers. They had there a, you know, they were fighting. And they had there somewhere the-- the poor or whatever. So I went to the movie. I never will know what they played, because, all the time, I thought all kind of questions what they can ask me and what should I answer, and what they could believe, what they wouldn't believe. I didn't see the-- the movie.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came out from the movie, and I start to go back. But then I got to the road, up to the sanatorium. Again, I heard that somebody is following me. It was heavy steps. I know that this he's a soldier. You-- you can hear on the steps, you know, how they walking. And they caught up with me and ask-- it was pitch dark. I didn't see who he was. And he said in German, [GERMAN]? Where are you running in-- in this dark? I said, I-- I live up in the sanatorium. And I talk German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Schwabes, they-- they're talking a dialect. And he said, oh, I-- how nice to hear that somebody speaks German and not Schwabeish And if you-- if you don't mind, I take you to the door that you have not to walk in this dark alone. It is not good to-- for a young lady to walk alone. In this minute, I thought, God sent this soldier to me. This is a God-sent angel, or whatever. They will save me. So I said, OK. And we went up. And at the gate was the biggest Nazi. He looked at me. These two come-- then I looked-- who is-- with whom I came. It was a very good looking, tall, young, blonde soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's cut. Just take us two minutes. We'll--
